title: Part 13
text: inside the cathedral. He had some bales of old, dirty newspaper that he used to take everywhere, and he started setting it on fire and throwing it down onto people. ‘How terrible.’ ‘I know. Frightening, isn’t it? When we — I mean the police — got there, it was evening — all dim and dark with flaming sheets of newspaper floating about and people dashing every- where with fire extinguishers and buckets of sand. Raphael and another guy tried to get to Pinny Wheeller, but when they were in the stairwell — which was a really tight, confined space — Pinny threw a load more burning newspaper down and some of it wrapped itself around the other guy’s face. So he had to go back.’ ‘But Raphael did not go back,’ I say, with great certainty. ‘No, she didn’t. Technically speaking she probably should have, but she didn’t. When she came out onto the gallery her hair was on fire. But, you know, she’s Raphael. I doubt she even noticed. The people down below had to shout at her to put the fire out. She sat down with Pinny Wheeller and she got him to stop throwing flaming newspaper everywhere and she got him to come down. Pretty brave, don’t you think?’ ‘Braver than you think. She doesn’t like heights.’ 236 ‘She doesn’t?’ ‘They make her uncomfortable.’ ‘That wouldn’t stop her,’ he says. ‘No.’ ‘Thank God, she didn’t have to do any of that with you. I mean she didn’t have to walk through fire or whatever. She just went to the seaside. That’s what I heard anyway — that she found you at the seaside.’ “Yes. I was at the side of the sea.’ ‘A lot of missing people turn up at seaside places,’ he muses. ‘It’s the sea, I suppose. It has a soothing effect.’ ‘It certainly did on me,’ I say. He smiles cheerfully at me. “Excellent, he says. Matthew Rose Sorensen has reappeared ENTRY FOR IR | NOVEMBER 2018 Matthew Rose Sorensen’s mother and father and sisters and friends all ask me where I have been. I tell them what I told Jamie Askill: that I was in a house with many rooms; that the sea sweeps through the house; and that sometimes it swept over me, but always I was saved. Matthew Rose Sorensen’s mother and father and sisters and friends tell each other that this is a description of a mental break- down seen from the inside; an explanation they find reasonable, perhaps even reassuring. They have Matthew Rose Sorensen back — or so they believe. A man with his face and voice and gestures moves about the world, and that is enough for them. 237 I no longer look like Piranesi. There are no coral beads or fishbones in my hair. My hair is clean and cut and styled. I am clean-shaven. I wear the clothes that were brought to me out of the storage in which Matthew Rose Sorensen’s sisters had placed them. Rose Sorensen had a great number of clothes, all meticulously cared for. He had more than a dozen suits (which I find surprising considering that his income was not large). This love of clothes was something he shared with Piranesi. Piranesi frequently wrote about Dr Ketterley’s clothes in his journal and lamented the contrast with his own ragged garments. This, I suppose, is where I differ from both of them — from Matthew Rose Sorensen and Piranesi; I find I do not care greatly about clothes. Many other things were delivered to me out of storage, the most important being Matthew Rose Sorensen’s missing journals. They cover the period from June 2000 (when he was an undergraduate) until December 2011. As for the rest of his possessions, I am getting rid of most of them. Piranesi cannot bear to have so many possessions. J do not need this! is his constant refrain. Piranesi is always with me, but of Rose Sorensen I have only hints and shadows. I piece him together out of the objects he has left behind, from what is said about him by other people and, of course, from his journals. Without the journals I would be all at sea. I remember how this world works — more or less. I remem- ber what Manchester is and what the police are and how to use a smartphone. I can pay for things with money — though I still find the process strange and artificial. Piranesi has a strong 238 dislike of money. Piranesi wants to say: But J need the thing you have, so why don 't you just give it to me? And then when I have some- thing you need, I will just give it to you. This would be a simpler system and much better! But I, who am not Piranesi — or at least not only him — real- ise that this probably wouldn’t go down too well. I have decided to write a book about Laurence Arne-Sayles. It is something that Matthew Rose Sorensen wanted to do and something that I want to do. After all, who knows Arne- Sayles’s work better than me? Raphael has shown me what Laurence Arne-Sayles taught her: how to find the path to the labyrinth and how to find the path out again. I can come and go as I please. Last week I took a train to Manchester. I took a bus to Miles Platting. I walked through a bleak autumn landscape to a flat in a tower block. The door was answered by a thin, ravaged-looking man who smelt strongly of cigarettes. ‘Are you James Ritter?’ I asked. He agreed that he was. ‘I’ve come to take you back,’ I said. I led him through the shadowy corridor and when the noble minotaurs of the first vestibule rose up around us, he started to cry, not for fear, but for happiness. He went immediately and sat under the great marble sweep of the staircase; the place where he used to sleep. He closed his eyes and listened to the sounds of the tides. When it was time to leave, he begged me to let him stay, but I refused. ‘You don’t know how to feed yourself, I told him. ‘You never learnt. You would die here unless I fed you — and I can’t 239 take on that responsibility. But I’ll bring you back here when- ever you want. And if ever I decide to come back for good, I promise I will bring you with me,’ The body of Valentine Ketterley, magician and scientist ENTRY FOR 28 NOVEMBER 2018 The body of Valentine Ketterley, magician and scientist, is washed by the tides. I have placed it in one of the lower halls accessed from the eighth vestibule and I have tethered it to the statue of a half-reclining man. The statue’s eyes are closed; he is possibly asleep; thick snakes and serpents entwine them- selves heavily with his limbs. The body is contained in a sack of plastic netting. The mesh of the netting is wide enough for fish to poke their mouths in, and birds their beaks; it is fine enough that none of the small bones will be lost. I estimate that in six months’ time the bones will be white and clean. I will gather them up and take them to the empty niche in the third north-western hall. I will place Valentine Ketterley next to the biscuit-box man. In the middle I will place the long bones tied together with twine. On the right I will place the skull. On the left I will place a box containing all the small bones. Dr Valentine Ketterley will lie with his colleagues: with Stanley Ovenden, Maurizio Giussani and Sylvia D’ Agostino. 240 Statues again ENTRY FOR 29 NOVEMBER 2018 Piranesi lived among statues: silent presences that brought him comfort and enlightenment. I thought that in this new (old) world the statues would be irrelevant. I did not imagine that they would continue to help me. But I was wrong. When faced with a person or situation I do not understand, my first impulse is still to look for a statue that will enlighten me. I think of Dr Ketterley and an image rises up in my mind. It is the memory ofa statue that stands in the nineteenth north-west- ern hall. It is the statue of a man kneeling on his plinth; a sword lies at his side, its blade broken in five pieces. Roundabout lie other broken pieces, the remains of a sphere. The man has used his sword to shatter the sphere because he wanted to under- stand it, but now he finds that he has destroyed both sphere and sword. This puzzles him, but at the same time part of him refuses to accept that the sphere is broken and worthless. He has picked up some of the fragments and stares at them intently in the hope that they will eventually bring him new knowledge. I think of Laurence Arne-Sayles and an image rises up in my mind. It is the memory of a statue that stands in an upper vesti- bule, facing the head of a staircase (the one rising up out of the thirty-second vestibule). This statue represents a heretical pope seated on a throne. He is fat and bloated. He lolls on his throne, a shapeless mass. The throne is magnificent, but the sheer bulk of the figure threatens to split it in two. He 241 knows that he is repulsive, but you can see by his face that the idea pleases him. He revels in the thought that he is somehow shocking. In his face there is mingled laughter and triumph. Look at me, he seems to say. Look at me! I think of Raphael and an image — no, two images rise up in my mind. In Piranesi’s mind Raphael is represented by a statue in the forty-fourth western hall. It shows a queen in a chariot, the protector of her people. She is all goodness, all gentleness, all wisdom, all motherhood. That is Piranesi’s view of Raphael, because Raphael saved him. But I choose a different statue. In my mind Raphael is better represented by a statue in an antechamber that lies between the forty-fifth and the sixty-second northern halls. This statue shows a figure walking forward, holding a lantern. It is hard to determine with any certainty the gender of the figure; it is androgynous in appearance. From the way she (or he) holds up the lantern and peers at whatever is ahead, one gets the sense of a huge darkness surrounding her; above all I get the sense that she is alone, perhaps by choice or perhaps because no one else was courageous enough to follow her into the darkness. Of all the billions of people in this world Raphael is the one I know best and love most. I understand much better now — better than Piranesi ever could — the magnificent thing she did in coming to find me, the magnitude of her courage. I know that she returns to the labyrinth often. Sometimes we go together; sometimes she goes alone. The quiet and the solitude attract her strongly. In them she hopes to find what she needs. 242 It worries me. ‘Don’t disappear, I tell her sternly. ‘Do not disappear.’ She makes a rueful, amused face. ‘I won’t,’ she says. ‘We can’t keep rescuing each other,’ I say. ‘It’s ridiculous.’ She smiles. It is a smile with a little sadness in it. But she still wears the perfume — the first thing I ever knew of her — and it still makes me think of Sunlight and Happiness. In my mind are all the tides ENTRY FOR 30 NOVEMBER 2018 In my mind are all the tides, their seasons, their ebbs and their flows. In my mind are all the halls, the endless procession of them, the intricate pathways. When this world becomes too much for me, when I grow tired of the noise and the dirt and the people, I close my eyes and I name a particular vestibule to myself; then I name a hall. I imagine I am walking the path from the vestibule to the hall. I note with precision the doors I must pass through, the rights and lefts that I must take, the statues on the walls that I must pass. Last night I dreamt that I was standing in the fifth north- ern hall facing the statue of the gorilla. The gorilla dismounted from his plinth and came towards me with his slow knuckle- walk. He was grey-white in the moonlight; and I flung my arms around his massive neck and told him how happy I was to be home. When I awoke I thought: J am not home. I am here. 243 It began to snow ENTRY FOR I DECEMBER 20I 8 This afternoon I walked through the city, making for a café where I was to meet Raphael. It was about half-past two on a day that had never really got light. It began to snow. The low clouds made a grey ceiling for the city; the snow muffled the noise of the cars until it became almost rhythmical; a steady, shushing noise, like the sound of tides beating endlessly on marble walls. I closed my eyes. I felt calm. There was a park. I entered it and followed a path through an avenue of tall, ancient trees with wide, dusky, grassy spaces on either side of them. The pale snow sifted down through bare winter branches. The lights of the cars on the distant road spar- kled through the trees: red, yellow, white. It was very quiet. Though it was not yet twilight the streetlights shed a faint light. People were walking up and down on the path. An old man passed me. He looked sad and tired. He had broken veins on his cheeks and a bristly white beard. As he screwed up his eyes against the falling snow, I realised I knew him. He is depicted on the northern wall of the forty-eighth western hall. He is shown as a king with a little model of a walled city in one hand while the other hand he raises in blessing. I wanted to seize hold of him and say to him: In another world you are a king, noble and good! I have seen it! But I hesitated a moment too long and he disappeared into the crowd. A woman passed me with two children. One of the children had a wooden recorder in his hands. I knew them too. They are 244 depicted in the twenty-seventh southern hall: a statue of two children laughing, one of them holding a flute. I came out of the park. The city streets rose up around me. There was a hotel with a courtyard with metal tables and chairs for people to sit in more clement weather. Today they were snow-strewn and forlorn. A lattice of wire was strung across the courtyard. Paper lanterns were hanging from the wires, spheres of vivid orange that blew and trembled in the snow and the thin wind; the sea-grey clouds raced across the sky and the orange lanterns shivered against them. The Beauty of the House is immeasurable; its Kindness infinite. 245 50.21 jn, ph ial Sapp robes, Ma aeaaineae i 2 Des | Pele ies a) are ed! (Fragment of text chalked on the Pavement of the Sixth North-Western Hall and discovered on the Seventeenth Day of the Ninth Month in the Year the Albatross came to the South-Western Halls) > : _ iy — aa alt Ler eT ' - a ra er nif —» x 7 i a aan 2 Wal wie. GWE | a. cm - ~<a ta TT ae Ns 29 Rp @ st be jpedagrabdig ®t taal OW) hie are, me om bypaeregh | ~ rt hae eat Wk al tO rhe i" First Interview: Hannah Priestley, Cartographer, 1 August 2018 ‘ ... kay. So that’s recording. Yes. So we’ Il start, if that’s OK. So my name’s Detective Sergeant Sarah Raphael. And I’m talking to Hannah Priestley.’ ‘Should I say something?’ ‘If you like. It’s just an informal chat. The recording’s just for me, for my notes. It doesn’t mean that we suspect you of anything,’ AG) ee ‘Because we absolutely don’t.’ ‘Understood,’ ‘Good. OK, then. Well, it’s been suggested that I talk to you because you're a friend of Dr Hubert? Is that right?’ ‘Ummm. No. | don’t think so. Who’s Dr Hubert?’ ‘Oh! OK. Good start. I thought ... Dr Hubert is Dr Ketterley’s ex-wife? Dr Valentine Ketterley? Are you sure ... ?’ ‘Oh! Yes. Yes, I have heard of her. But I’ve never met her,’ ‘But you know Dr Ketterley?’ ‘I ... Yes. I knew him briefly. It was .... Oh God, when was it? A few years ago now.’ Pause. ‘Um, is this about ... 7’ ‘About ... ?’ ‘Well ... Now that you’ve mentioned Dr Ketterley’s ex-wife ...’ ‘Dr Hubert.’ ‘Yes. I know that a while back Dr Hubert was going around telling people that Val was stalking me. Is it about that?’ Pause. ‘Well, yes. I suppose so. A couple of people we’ve spoken to have said that they believed Dr Ketterley may have stalked you or behaved in a way that might be interpreted as stalking. So what we want to do nowis ... ’ ‘It isn’t true.’ ‘Oh! Right. OK. Good to know.’ ‘I never got the least sign from him that he was interested in me in that way. If anything I thought he was rather bored.’ ‘OK. Well, perhaps you could say something about how you knew him. I’m interested to know why these other people thought he was stalking you. How did you meet?’ ‘Through a mutual friend. Liz. We were both at a dinner party at Liz’s house, seated next to each other. And we had the usual sort of conversation. How do you know Liz? — what do you do? — what sort of interests do you have? That sort of thing. As I say he didn’t exactly seem riveted by my company. But afterwards he called me and said he wanted to commission me to do some work.’ ‘And did you do it? The work? Sorry, I don’t actually know what it is that you do,’ ‘I’m a cartographer. I make maps.’ ‘Oh, really! That sounds interesting’ ‘It is. Very.’ ‘So ... ? Dr Ketterley wanted you to make a map?’ ‘Yes.’ ‘Of what?’ ‘He didn’t say. Not at the beginning. He told me that he wanted me to go with him to some unspecified place and then I could make a map of it.’ ‘OK. Well, I’m beginning to see why people thought he was stalking you. What did you say?’ ‘I can’t really remember. | think I was a bit flummoxed. It’s not what I do. When I make maps I use data that someone else has provided. The data might be geographical features, street grids, whatever’s required. I don’t do any initial surveying. There are all sorts of easily accessible apps and databases and a lot of the data is free. I think I explained all this and then I said, “Just tell me where the place is and what sort of map you want and I'll find the data.” ‘And what did he say?’ ‘There was a long silence. And then he said, “There is no data ‘Could he have been talking about somewhere remote?’ ”) ‘No. It was near London. He said we could be there and back in a day.’ ‘OK. Let me see if I’ve understood. He wanted you to make a map of somewhere that was less than a day’s travel from London, but at the same time he was claiming there were no maps of this place at all?’ ‘Exactly. It made no sense. I wanted to end the call at that point, but he switched tactics and said we should meet up and then he’d be able to explain better. So we made an arrange- ment to have dinner at a place near the Barbican. I was quite nervous because the whole thing seemed so peculiar, but I went anyway. And when I got there he told me it was a maze. He wanted me to make a map of a maze. So as far as I was concerned that threw the whole project into a different light. I’ve always loved mazes and labyrinths. You can make such beautiful maps of them. Straightaway I was on board. I asked if he was sure that a plan of the maze didn’t exist somewhere. He said that it was possible that someone had made a map in the past, but he had no way of accessing it. He said he’d tried making a map of it himself, but it hadn’t worked. The layout was too complicated; he couldn’t get his head round it. I asked him if he’d discovered the way through it. And he said it wasn’t about finding a way through, it was about what’s inside.’ ‘And what was that?’ ‘Statues, he said. And possibly other things. He said there were parts of it he hadn’t explored. I asked him if anyone else would be there. And he said no, it would be completely deserted. So then we starting making plans for our trip, and he said...’ ‘Hold on a minute. By this time he’d told you where it was?’ ‘No.’ “You still didn’t know where it was?’ ‘No, but that didn’t bother me any more. You see I’d worked out what was going on. This place — wherever it was — it must have been like Burnt Norton.’ ‘Like Burnt Norton?’ ‘Yes.’ ‘Sorry. I’ve no idea what that means.’ ‘Burnt Norton was a garden. I mean, it still is I suppose. T. S. Eliot went there and he wrote a poem about it. But he wasn’t supposed to be there. He didn’t have permission or anything. He just trespassed. And then he produced an extraordinary poem. And I think something similar happened to Val. He’d discovered a hedge maze in an old garden — the garden of a derelict house where no one ever went. I think he must have asked the owners of the garden if he could examine it and document it and they’d said no. But he’d gone anyway. That was why he didn’t want to say where it was. In case the owners found out he was going there.’ AS) ee ‘After our meeting at the restaurant I thought a lot about it. I could see it so clearly in my mind. The maze and the deserted garden. The quiet and the birdsong. And I was really excited to map it. I already knew what typefaces I was going to use, how I was going to draw it. But it was more than that. I can’t explain it very well, but I felt like something was waiting for me there. And I really, really wanted to go there.’ ‘What sort of thing?’ ‘Peace, I suppose. I felt like peace was waiting for me there.’ ‘Well, that’s ... I mean peace is always good.’ Laughs. ‘It’s silly. I know it’s silly. So, as I said, we started making plans. Val said to wear old clothes and strong shoes. I suppose the garden had got a bit overgrown. We were going to take lunch with us and Val said to wrap up warm because it got cold. We set a date. We were going to meet at his house. But a few days before we were due to go he rang and cancelled.’ ‘Did he say why?’ ‘He said he’d found someone else.’ ‘Another cartographer?’ ‘He said it wasn’t a cartographer. It was someone who could get other kinds of information about the maze.’ ‘What information?’ ‘T honestly have no clue. I said something like: “I thought the whole idea was to make a map.” And he said that was one of the ideas, but not the only one.’ ‘What do you think that meant?’ ‘I don’t know. Honestly I was so pissed off.’ ‘I don’t blame you.’ ‘He’d just been wasting my time. And I’d been so looking forward to making my map.’ Silence. ‘And he definitely said that it was a maze?’ Pause. ‘Actually I think the word he used was “labyrinth”. But it’s the same thing, isn’t it?’ ‘And you said that you set a date to visit the maze. Would you be able to find out when that was?’ ‘Probably. It’ll be in my calendar somewhere. It was around the end of 2012.’ ‘If you could let me have that, it would be really helpful.’ Pause. ‘! don’t suppose Dr Ketterley happened to mention the name of this other person? The one who was getting him the information?’ ‘No.’ ‘Did he ever mention a man called Matthew Rose Sorensen?’ ‘No.’ “You're sure?’ ‘Yes.’ Second interview: Al Martineau, field recordist, 10 August 2018 ‘ ... if you ask someone at the front desk, they should be able to sort you out. OK. So my name’s Detective Sergeant Sarah Raphael. And I’m talking to Albert Martineau. Should I call you Albert or Al?’ ‘Al’s fine.’ ‘Perfect. What this is about is that in 2011 you saw Dr Ketterley as part of ... of treatment of some kind?’ ‘Treatment for depression. Yes.’ ‘So I'd like to talk to you about that and obviously we might be treading close to some personal matters and I need to stress again that you don’t need to talk about anything that makes you uncomfortable. And you don’t have to talk to me at all if you don’t want to.’ ‘It’s fine. I don’t mind talking about it.’ ‘Great. Thank you. The reason we’re interested in talking to you is because of an email you wrote on 21 November 2011 to the Psychiatric Services at St Dymphna’s Hospital in Ealing... ’ “Yes,” ‘... and in this email you expressed some concern about Dr Ketterley’s mode of therapy. Have I got that right?’ ‘Yes. But it’s important to say that about a week later I wrote a second email taking back what I'd said in the first email. I apologised for wasting their time.’ ‘Right. Would you mind telling me a little about what led up to you writing the first email?’ ‘Sure. I mean ... OK. It wasn’t anything really. It was just strange, I suppose. You don’t expect a therapist to do that. But I don’t want to get him into trouble. What’s this about exactly?’ ‘We’re looking into a disappearance. A disappearance which is possibly connected to several other disappearances.’ ‘Oh, OK. And this has something to do with Val?’ ‘Probably not. That’s what we’re trying to find out. He knew some of the people who disappeared.’ ‘I see. Well, the time you're talking about ... the reason I was seeing him was that I was going through a really bad time. I'd done something very stupid. I’d got involved with a woman who was a friend of my sister and who was married. I was twenty-four and she was forty-three. I thought it was this great love affair and it turned out ... it turned out it wasn’t. And in one fell swoop all my confidence flew out of the window. I’d hurt my family and I'd really misjudged the other person. She wasn’t who I thought she was. It was a huge shock. I started having panic attacks. I couldn’t concentrate on anything. I lost my job. And so on and so forth.’ Pause. ‘I’d been to a couple of private therapists and that hadn’t worked out and then my GP referred me to the Psychiatric Services at St Dymphna’s and they sent me to Val. And I quite liked him. At least I liked him to begin with. He was very cool, very professional and by that point I’d had enough of warm, friendly people. Warm, friendly people betrayed you. That was what | thought. So I found his detachment quite soothing, I suppose. Our sessions went pretty well and then one time I mentioned that I was interested in field recordings. It’s a big thing of mine. And Val said ... ’ ‘Sorry, what? Field what?’ ‘Recordings. It’s where you take sound recording equip- ment and you go out and make recordings of whatever’s going on in the world.’ ‘Like bird recordings?’ ‘Could be birds. That’s usually the part people are most familiar with. But it needn’t be birds. It could be anything. A suspension bridge in a high wind. A garden on a hot day with
